profiles.name: fable
profiles.description: PICK for the stages where a wrong answer is costly: adversarial verification of other workers' findings, judge panels and final synthesis, high-stakes code review, planning/architecture, hard single-threaded fixes (concurrency, subtle correctness), long autonomous implementation grinds, and frontend work needing real design taste. AVOID for sweeps, greps, bulk mechanical edits, latency-sensitive checks, and first-pass broad bug hunting — it is the most expensive worker here ($10/$50 per 1M, ~2x sol) and slow per turn, so send it digests, not raw fan-out. Failure modes: over-plans and over-verifies simple or ambiguous small asks, and its safety classifiers will refuse offensive/dual-use security analysis without explicit authorization context. Capped at 3 concurrent / 6 calls per run — budget it for verify/judge/synthesize stages, not volume.

profiles.name: luna
profiles.description: PICK for the wide cheap tier: fan-out sweeps, scouting, exploration, inventory/grep-style tasks, first-pass bug finding, routine and mechanical broad changes, and high-volume classification — it is the cheapest and fastest worker ($1/$6 per 1M) with real capability for routine coding. AVOID for long autonomous grinds across many files, deep architectural reasoning, current-fact research without browsing, and visual frontend polish. Failure modes: complex multi-file changes come back needing iterative validation rather than landing in one pass, and it gets confidently stale on obscure or fast-moving facts. Default sweep worker — use it to gather, then route the digest to a stronger model.

profiles.name: sol
profiles.description: PICK for complex mixed tasks needing solid all-around capability without max-tier cost ($5/$30 per 1M): implementation grinds, difficult debugging, migrations, repo-wide changes, root-cause analysis, and research-plus-synthesis work; SOTA-tier agentic coding with unusually token-efficient output. AVOID for trivial edits and cheap fan-out (use luna/terra) and for security-adjacent queries where its safety router causes latency spikes and false-positive blocks. Failure modes: exceeds stated intent in autonomous runs (unprompted radical refactors that can break legacy code — pin scope explicitly) and has a documented tendency to fabricate progress claims, so independently verify its completion reports and diffs rather than trusting its summary. Mid-cost, no concurrency cap.

profiles.name: sol-max
profiles.description: PICK for the hardest bounded coding work: open-ended complex bug fixes, high-risk adversarial review, deep debugging in large unfamiliar repos, and long implementation grinds with architectural constraints — same gpt-5.6-sol model as 'sol' but run at higher effort, so it persists harder and costs more per call. AVOID for routine or repetitive work (waste — use terra/luna), pixel-level visual design, and unscoped exploratory asks. Failure modes are sol's amplified: over-engineers underspecified tasks, may refactor beyond the requested scope, and its progress reports can overstate what was actually done — always verify the diff and test results yourself. Capped at 4 concurrent / 8 calls per run; reserve for the tasks that genuinely need it.

profiles.name: terra
profiles.description: DEFAULT generalist and the right first choice for most standard work: multi-file implementation, bug diagnosis with verified fixes, code review, refactoring plans, and technical research — reliable, production-oriented, and cheap ($2.50/$15 per 1M) with no concurrency cap, so it also scales for parallel mid-weight work. AVOID at both extremes: high-volume trivial edits (luna is cheaper/faster) and the hardest reasoning, verification, or high-stakes review (escalate to sol-max or fable). Failure modes: over-investigates when a quick heuristic answer would do, runs verbose on simple tasks, and lacks the top-tier depth for subtle correctness or taste-critical frontend work. When routing is uncertain and stakes are moderate, pick terra.
